# Article 127 — Provisions for Facilitating Company Reconstruction or Amalgamation

Article 127 of the **[Companies (Jersey) Law 1991][cjl]** **arms
the Royal Court** with broad powers, in a scheme of arrangement
that is for the purpose of a **reconstruction** or
**amalgamation**, to effect the transfer of property, liabilities,
contracts and employees from one company to another **by court
order**.

## What Article 127 covers

Where the Court is satisfied that the compromise or arrangement
proposed under Article 125 is for the purpose of:

- A **scheme for the reconstruction** of any company; or
- A **scheme for the amalgamation** of any two or more companies;

and that property or liabilities of any company concerned are to
be transferred to another company, the Court may make provision
in its order for:

- The **transfer of property and liabilities** (including
  intangibles, contracts, and book debts);
- The **allotment of shares, debentures or like interests** in
  the transferee company to the members of the transferor
  company;
- The **continuation by or against the transferee** of any legal
  proceedings pending by or against any transferor;
- The **dissolution** (without winding up) of any transferor
  company;
- **Such incidental, consequential and supplemental matters** as
  are necessary to secure the reconstruction or amalgamation.

(See the consolidated text at [jerseylaw.je][cjl].)

## Why this matters

A **conventional asset transfer** between companies requires
individual transfers of every asset, novation of every contract,
TUPE-style transfer of employees, and re-grant of every consent.
For complex groups or large operating businesses, this is
impractical.

Article 127 lets the **Royal Court order all of it at once** as
part of a scheme. The court order is a **universal title
transfer** — third parties are bound, assets pass automatically,
and contracts continue with the transferee.

## Typical reconstruction / amalgamation uses

- **Group simplification** — moving operating subsidiaries up,
  consolidating businesses across multiple legal entities;
- **Cross-border consolidation** — combining Jersey companies
  with other-jurisdiction entities (often via dual schemes or via
  Part 18B merger as an alternative);
- **Internal reorganisation** ahead of an IPO or trade sale;
- **Hive-up / hive-across** of operating businesses.

## Relationship with Part 18B Mergers

The statutory **merger regime** in Part 18B (Articles 127A onwards)
provides another route to similar outcomes — a court-free merger
between Jersey companies (or with foreign companies under
specified conditions). Which route to choose depends on:

- **Whether court oversight is wanted** (yes → Article 127 scheme;
  no → Part 18B merger);
- **Whether creditors need to be compromised** (yes → scheme);
- **Cross-border counterparty** (some foreign jurisdictions
  recognise schemes more readily; others recognise mergers).
